coherence.justification: Three paragraphs are present, but Body 1 has no clear topic sentence and drifts across four millennia. Mechanical repetition of "*People wanted… People wanted… people finally invented*" is exactly the Band 6→7 pattern flagged in `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §2. Demonstratives lack anchors — "*that shone*", "*That strong will*", "*By doing that*", "*This was developed even further*" each force the reader to infer the antecedent (`cohesion-upgrades.md` §9 "demonstrative without anchor"). The truncation removes any inter-paragraph synthesis. Sentences are not fluently linked → Band 5.

lexical.justification: Topic-relevant items present: "*electronic signals*", "*binary digits*", "*hardware*", "*base-60*", "*switches*". But word-choice density is the textbook Band-6 risk-taker signature: "*new logics*" (uncountable misuse, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §B2), "*mathematics is inevitable*" (wrong collocation — should be *indispensable / essential*), "*the first ever information*" (information ≠ message), "*a single hardware*" (uncountable), "*go up to almost countless numbers*" (vague). Meaning is generally clear, so Band 6 — not Band 5 — is correct.

grammar.justification: Several complex attempts break: "*Computers consist of a full of small switches*" (broken determiner), "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" (missing auxiliary; *dove* singular/wrong noun, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §I.2 fragment, §B1 plural), "*not to mention the Babylonians used base-60*" (Korean L1 calque, §K). Simple sentences ("*This was developed even further*", "*The graphic on the screen is just a number as well*") are mostly accurate, fitting the Band-5 "greatest accuracy on simple sentences". Errors do cause the reader to pause in two places — Band-5 indicator.

overall_rationale: The four criteria average 5.25, which rounds up to **5.5** per the `.25 → .5` rule in `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §3. Vocabulary risk-taking holds LR at 6, but missing thesis + missing conclusion + period drift + faulty complex sentences keep TR/CC/GRA at 5 until structural repair lands. The 6→7 target the candidate is aiming for is currently a 5.5→7 gap — closeable, but it needs the structural fix above before any sentence-level rewrite can take effect.
what_changed: Iter 11 correctly recalibrated against the recovered homework prompt; I keep its 5/5/6/5 profile and its "stay inside one period" diagnosis, but sharpen three things. **First**, I make the word-count gap explicit: 290 / ~500 requested is *worse* than the 250-word IELTS floor relative to the assignment, so TR cannot reach 6 by content alone (`contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §4). **Second**, I retire the "concession with engineering/physics" rewrite that earlier iterations imported from the wrong prompt — under the real homework, the candidate just needs to demonstrate *how* maths influenced the period, not argue it was *the most important* driver. **Third**, I push two specific grammar showcases the prior chain has under-deployed under the corrected prompt: a `Were … to …` inverted Type-2 conditional (`contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §9) and an `Adj + as + S + V` concessive (§4), which together cover both bodies cleanly.

rewrites.original: In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science.
rewrites.improved: Building on numerical traditions reaching back to Babylonian base-60, the computer revolution of the twentieth century rested on three concrete mathematical foundations: Boolean logic, binary representation and the numerical encoding of all information.
rewrites.reason: Compresses ancient history into one subordinate clause (period control) and replaces the topic announcement with a thesis naming three mechanisms — gating fix for TR.

rewrites.original: (essay ends abruptly at "*…just a number as well.*" — no conclusion)
rewrites.improved: Taken together, these examples show that mathematics did not merely assist the computer revolution; it supplied the very language — binary, logical, encoded — in which computation became possible at all.
rewrites.reason: Adds the missing synthesis conclusion (`paragraph-structure-band7.md` §5); a 2-sentence conclusion is required to clear Band 5 TR.

rewrites.original: Computers consist of a full of small switches which can turn on and off.
rewrites.improved: At the heart of every modern computer lies an array of tiny switches whose two states map directly onto the 0 and 1 of Boolean algebra.
rewrites.reason: Repairs the broken "a full of"; introduces a place-fronted full inversion (Band-7 GRA showcase) and names Boolean algebra — the missing mathematical mechanism.

rewrites.original: If it has billions and trillions of switches, then it can go up to almost countless numbers.
rewrites.improved: Were a single processor to contain billions of such switches, it could in principle represent any value expressible in binary, however large.
rewrites.reason: Replaces the childlike `If…then` with an inverted Type-2 conditional + hedging — one of the highest-leverage Band-7 grammar showcases per character of effort.

rewrites.original: The reason it matters is because every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number.
rewrites.improved: What makes this binary substrate so powerful is the principle of encoding: every character, image and instruction the machine handles is ultimately reduced to a sequence of integers.
rewrites.reason: Pseudo-cleft (`What … is …`) emphasises the mechanism; corrects the "*reason… is because*" redundancy; introduces the precise term *encoding*.

rewrites.original: so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed
rewrites.improved: so that messages no longer had to be carried by human couriers or carrier pigeons
rewrites.reason: Fixes missing passive auxiliary (*were*), the singular "*dove*" countability slip, and restores natural collocation.

rewrites.original: People have built up mathematical rules by defining new logics to solve problems.
rewrites.improved: Modest as these early efforts were, mathematicians steadily built systems of formal logic that turned vague problems into precisely solvable ones.
rewrites.reason: Fixes "*new logics*" (uncountable misuse); names the mechanism (formal logic); deploys an `Adj + as + S + V` concessive — the cheapest single Band-7 GRA upgrade.
Replaces a wrong-collocation (*inevitable* describes events, not tools) with the precise band-7 collocation.

rewrites.original: a machine which can do several things in a single hardware, a computer.
rewrites.improved: a general-purpose machine — what we now call a computer — capable of executing any procedure that can be expressed as an algorithm
rewrites.reason: Fixes uncountable "*hardware*"; promotes the appositive with em-dashes; introduces the precise term *algorithm*.

rewrites.original: By doing that, it can calculate in binary digits.
rewrites.improved: By switching between these two states, the machine performs all of its calculations in binary.
rewrites.reason: Fixes the unclear "*that*" antecedent (`cohesion-upgrades.md` §9); replaces vague reference with a concrete lexical chain (*switches → these two states*).

convergence_note: This is only the second iteration since the prompt was corrected (iter 11 was the first), and substantive sharpenings are still landing: the explicit 290 / ~500-word gap framing, the retirement of the "concession with engineering/physics" rewrite that came from the wrong prompt, and two specific showcase-grammar drop-ins (`Were a processor to contain…`, `Modest as these early efforts were…`) that the chain had not yet placed under the corrected task. One more iteration could still test whether period drift alone, given the homework framing, justifies dropping CC to 4.5 territory or whether 5 is the stable read.
raw_markdown:
# Iteration 12 — claude

_Generated 2026-05-10T15:23:44Z. Prior chain depth: up to 20. Manifest: 122 files._

---I have enough corpus context. Now producing iteration 12. Iteration 11 just recalibrated against the recovered homework prompt; this iteration sharpens period control, word-count gap (290 vs 500 requested), and pushes deeper showcase-grammar upgrades.

---

## Per-criterion scores
| Criterion | Band | Descriptor evidence (file path) |
|---|---|---|
| Task Response / Achievement | 5.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5 ("incompletely addressed"; position underdeveloped; truncated) |
| Coherence and Cohesion       | 5.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5; `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §9 (demonstrative-without-anchor) |
| Lexical Resource             | 6.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 ("risk-taker" clause) |
| Grammatical Range & Accuracy | 5.0 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5 ("complex sentences attempted but faulty") |
| **Overall**                  | 5.5 | (5+5+6+5)/4 = 5.25 → 5.5 per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §3 |

## What changed from prior feedback
Iter 11 correctly recalibrated against the recovered homework prompt; I keep its 5/5/6/5 profile and its "stay inside one period" diagnosis, but sharpen three things. **First**, I make the word-count gap explicit: 290 / ~500 requested is *worse* than the 250-word IELTS floor relative to the assignment, so TR cannot reach 6 by content alone (`contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §4). **Second**, I retire the "concession with engineering/physics" rewrite that earlier iterations imported from the wrong prompt — under the real homework, the candidate just needs to demonstrate *how* maths influenced the period, not argue it was *the most important* driver. **Third**, I push two specific grammar showcases the prior chain has under-deployed under the corrected prompt: a `Were … to …` inverted Type-2 conditional (`contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §9) and an `Adj + as + S + V` concessive (§4), which together cover both bodies cleanly.

## Structural feedback
The essay does choose a workable historical period — "the computer revolution" — but does not stay inside it. Body 1 spends roughly half its words outside the chosen period (Mesopotamia, Egypt, Babylonian base-60, Morse code 1844), and the actual maths-on-computer-science content ("binary digits", "every letter is actually a number") is compressed into a single ~70-word paragraph that breaks off mid-explanation. There is **no thesis** ("In this essay, I will discuss…" is a topic announcement, not a position) and **no conclusion paragraph** — both gating issues for moving above Band 5 TR per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 7 indicators. The essay is also ~290 words against a ~500-word brief: under the homework rubric this is a content-development limiter, not a fixed penalty, but it makes Band 6 TR mathematically hard. **The single highest-impact fix is structural, not lexical:** keep the existing Body 2 idea (numerical encoding) as Body 2; add a *new* Body 1 anchored on Boolean logic → switching circuits *inside* the computer revolution (treat ancient examples as one-line backdrop only); and add a 2-sentence synthesis conclusion. The Mesopotamia/Babylonian/Morse material can survive as a single subordinate clause in the introduction ("Building on numerical traditions reaching back to Babylonian base-60…") rather than dominating Body 1. Per `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md` §3, each body paragraph then needs PEEL: claim → mechanism → named example → link. Doing only this — without changing one word of vocabulary — would lift TR and CC to Band 6.

## Focus areas
- **Period discipline (stay inside the computer revolution)** — Body 1's chronological drift to ancient Egypt/Mesopotamia/Morse code is the biggest TR leak; turn earlier history into one backdrop clause, not a paragraph. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md`, `contexts/08-vocabulary-grammar/04-task-response-error-correction-bank.md`.
- **PEEL with named mechanisms (Boolean logic, binary representation, algorithms, encoding)** — the essay names *no* mathematical concept; it gestures at maths-then-computers without explaining the link. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md` §3, `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-03-technology.md`, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/09-topic-vocabulary-clusters.md`.
- **Reference and lexical chains beyond bare "that"** — "that shone", "That strong will", "By doing that", "The reason it matters" all hinge on a vague *that*; build chains like *switches → these on/off states → such two-state devices*. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §3 Pillar 1 & §9, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/08-cohesion-upgrades-instructor.md`.
- **Articles + countability on uncountable/abstract nouns** — "new logics", "the first ever information", "a single hardware", "people or dove" are all article/countability slips Band 7 cannot tolerate. Drill: `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §A2, §B2, `contexts/08-vocabulary-grammar/01-grammar-error-correction-bank.md`.
- **One band-7 grammar showcase per body paragraph** — currently zero. The cheapest two are an inverted Type-2 conditional (`Were a machine to contain…`) and a concessive `Adj + as + S + V` (`Modest as these early codes seem,…`). Drill: `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §4, §9.
- **Length to ~500 words via developed bodies** — 290 words against a ~500-word brief leaves no room to extend ideas; expansion belongs in Bodies 1 and 2, not in a longer intro. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md` §1.

## What's working
The candidate is the Band-6 "risk-taker" the descriptor describes (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6), and the technical instinct is real and worth keeping: the dated specifics — "*Morse code in 1844*", "*the Babylonians used base-60 for timekeeping*" — and especially the conceptually correct chain "*every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number. The graphic on the screen is just a number as well*". That last chain is, in essence, the right thesis-mechanism for the whole essay; it should be promoted to the introduction and developed into a full PEEL paragraph rather than dropped as a one-liner.

## Per-criterion justification
**Task Response (5.0).** No thesis ("*In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science*" is a topic announcement, not a developed position) and no conclusion paragraph. Body 1's topic sentence ("*Humans have always wanted to preserve their thoughts somewhere to communicate*") is about communication history, not mathematics during the computer revolution. The 290-word length against a ~500-word brief means main ideas are necessarily underdeveloped. This fits Band 5 exactly: "main parts of the prompt are incompletely addressed… ideas put forward but limited and not sufficiently developed".

**Coherence and Cohesion (5.0).** Three paragraphs are present, but Body 1 has no clear topic sentence and drifts across four millennia. Mechanical repetition of "*People wanted… People wanted… people finally invented*" is exactly the Band 6→7 pattern flagged in `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §2. Demonstratives lack anchors — "*that shone*", "*That strong will*", "*By doing that*", "*This was developed even further*" each force the reader to infer the antecedent (`cohesion-upgrades.md` §9 "demonstrative without anchor"). The truncation removes any inter-paragraph synthesis. Sentences are not fluently linked → Band 5.

**Lexical Resource (6.0).** Topic-relevant items present: "*electronic signals*", "*binary digits*", "*hardware*", "*base-60*", "*switches*". But word-choice density is the textbook Band-6 risk-taker signature: "*new logics*" (uncountable misuse, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §B2), "*mathematics is inevitable*" (wrong collocation — should be *indispensable / essential*), "*the first ever information*" (information ≠ message), "*a single hardware*" (uncountable), "*go up to almost countless numbers*" (vague). Meaning is generally clear, so Band 6 — not Band 5 — is correct.

**Grammatical Range & Accuracy (5.0).** Several complex attempts break: "*Computers consist of a full of small switches*" (broken determiner), "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" (missing auxiliary; *dove* singular/wrong noun, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §I.2 fragment, §B1 plural), "*not to mention the Babylonians used base-60*" (Korean L1 calque, §K). Simple sentences ("*This was developed even further*", "*The graphic on the screen is just a number as well*") are mostly accurate, fitting the Band-5 "greatest accuracy on simple sentences". Errors do cause the reader to pause in two places — Band-5 indicator.

## Top concrete rewrites (5–10, ranked by band uplift)
| # | Original phrase from essay | Improved (band-7 target) | Reason | Corpus citation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science." | "**Building on numerical traditions reaching back to Babylonian base-60, the computer revolution of the twentieth century rested on three concrete mathematical foundations: Boolean logic, binary representation and the numerical encoding of all information.**" | Compresses ancient history into one subordinate clause (period control) and replaces the topic announcement with a thesis naming three mechanisms — gating fix for TR. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md` §2; `contexts/09-instructor-tips/06-task2-sentence-frames.md` |
| 2 | (essay ends abruptly at "*…just a number as well.*" — no conclusion) | "**Taken together, these examples show that mathematics did not merely assist the computer revolution; it supplied the very language — binary, logical, encoded — in which computation became possible at all.**" | Adds the missing synthesis conclusion (`paragraph-structure-band7.md` §5); a 2-sentence conclusion is required to clear Band 5 TR. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md` §5 |
| 3 | "Computers consist of a full of small switches which can turn on and off." | "**At the heart of every modern computer lies an array of tiny switches whose two states map directly onto the 0 and 1 of Boolean algebra.**" | Repairs the broken "a full of"; introduces a place-fronted full inversion (Band-7 GRA showcase) and names Boolean algebra — the missing mathematical mechanism. | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §3 (full inversion); `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` |
| 4 | "If it has billions and trillions of switches, then it can go up to almost countless numbers." | "**Were a single processor to contain billions of such switches, it could in principle represent any value expressible in binary, however large.**" | Replaces the childlike `If…then` with an inverted Type-2 conditional + hedging — one of the highest-leverage Band-7 grammar showcases per character of effort. | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §9 (inverted conditionals) |
| 5 | "The reason it matters is because every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number." | "**What makes this binary substrate so powerful is the principle of encoding: every character, image and instruction the machine handles is ultimately reduced to a sequence of integers.**" | Pseudo-cleft (`What … is …`) emphasises the mechanism; corrects the "*reason… is because*" redundancy; introduces the precise term *encoding*. | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §6 (pseudo-cleft); `contexts/09-instructor-tips/09-topic-vocabulary-clusters.md` |
| 6 | "so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed" | "**so that messages no longer had to be carried by human couriers or carrier pigeons**" | Fixes missing passive auxiliary (*were*), the singular "*dove*" countability slip, and restores natural collocation. | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §B1, §I.2 |
| 7 | "People have built up mathematical rules by defining new logics to solve problems." | "**Modest as these early efforts were, mathematicians steadily built systems of formal logic that turned vague problems into precisely solvable ones.**" | Fixes "*new logics*" (uncountable misuse); names the mechanism (formal logic); deploys an `Adj + as + S + V` concessive — the cheapest single Band-7 GRA upgrade. | `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` §4; `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §B2 |
| 8 | "mathematics is inevitable" | "**mathematical reasoning is indispensable**" | Replaces a wrong-collocation (*inevitable* describes events, not tools) with the precise band-7 collocation. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/vocabulary-upgrades.md`; `contexts/08-vocabulary-grammar/06-paraphrase-quick-lookup.md` |
| 9 | "a machine which can do several things in a single hardware, a computer." | "**a general-purpose machine — what we now call a computer — capable of executing any procedure that can be expressed as an algorithm**" | Fixes uncountable "*hardware*"; promotes the appositive with em-dashes; introduces the precise term *algorithm*. | `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-03-technology.md`; `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §B2 |
| 10 | "By doing that, it can calculate in binary digits." | "**By switching between these two states, the machine performs all of its calculations in binary.**" | Fixes the unclear "*that*" antecedent (`cohesion-upgrades.md` §9); replaces vague reference with a concrete lexical chain (*switches → these two states*). | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §3 Pillar 3 |

## Overall band rationale
The four criteria average 5.25, which rounds up to **5.5** per the `.25 → .5` rule in `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §3. Vocabulary risk-taking holds LR at 6, but missing thesis + missing conclusion + period drift + faulty complex sentences keep TR/CC/GRA at 5 until structural repair lands. The 6→7 target the candidate is aiming for is currently a 5.5→7 gap — closeable, but it needs the structural fix above before any sentence-level rewrite can take effect.

## CONVERGENCE: REFINING
This is only the second iteration since the prompt was corrected (iter 11 was the first), and substantive sharpenings are still landing: the explicit 290 / ~500-word gap framing, the retirement of the "concession with engineering/physics" rewrite that came from the wrong prompt, and two specific showcase-grammar drop-ins (`Were a processor to contain…`, `Modest as these early efforts were…`) that the chain had not yet placed under the corrected task. One more iteration could still test whether period drift alone, given the homework framing, justifies dropping CC to 4.5 territory or whether 5 is the stable read.
